ocr.text: In assessing the comparative performance of various regions, the data presented in the table highlights significant differences in
the number of samples collected across distinct geographic areas. The "No. Samples" column indicates that a total of 25
samples were obtained overall, with Wisconsin contributing 2 samples, while Martha’s Vineyard and Cape Cod together
accounted for 3. This disparity underscores the varying levels of engagement and resource availability for data collection across
these regions, which may influence the reliability and generalizability of findings.
Statistic  All  Wisconsin  Martha’s Vineyard/Cape Cod  Northeast
No. Samples  25  2  3  20
No. polymorphic (segregating) sites, S  167  42  4  21
No. haplotypes, h  17  2  3  12
Haplotype (gene) diversity, Hd  0.943  1  1  0.911
Std. dev, haplotype diversity  0.031  0.5  0.272  0.045
Nucleotide diversity, Pi  1.14E-03  1.47E-03  9.00E-05  1.60E-04
Watterson’s theta (per site)  1.55E-03  1.47E-03  8.00E-05  2.10E-04
Std. dev, Watterson’s theta  5.00E-04  1.10E-06  7.00E-05  8.00E-05
Furthermore, the Northeast region, encompassing both Wisconsin and Martha’s Vineyard/Cape Cod, reflects the broader
demographic and environmental contexts that may affect the outcomes of the study. As researchers continue to analyze these
statistics, it is essential to consider not only the quantity of samples but also the qualitative aspects that each location represents.
Understanding the context behind these metrics can provide deeper insights into the patterns observed, facilitating more
informed conclusions and recommendations in future studies. This analysis serves as a foundation for further exploration into
the underlying factors that drive regional differences and the implications of these variations on the overall study objectives.
